questions.question: Why does LightGBM's leaf-wise tree growth strategy make it significantly faster than XGBoost's level-wise strategy for large datasets?
options: LightGBM uses GPU acceleration while XGBoost only uses CPU.

Level-wise growth splits ALL leaf nodes at each depth level, even ones that provide minimal improvement. Leaf-wise growth selects the SINGLE leaf with the maximum potential gain to split next — this achieves lower training loss in fewer splits, requiring fewer trees to converge and reducing overall computation.
LightGBM uses smaller trees (depth 3) while XGBoost uses larger trees (depth 6).
LightGBM caches the training dataset in GPU memory; XGBoost reads from disk.
questions.answer: Level-wise growth splits ALL leaf nodes at each depth level, even ones that provide minimal improvement. Leaf-wise growth selects the SINGLE leaf with the maximum potential gain to split next — this achieves lower training loss in fewer splits, requiring fewer trees to converge and reducing overall computation.
questions.explanation: At depth 6, level-wise growth creates 64 splits per tree level regardless of their value. Leaf-wise growth creates only the highest-value split — making each tree more efficient and allowing LightGBM to train 10-100x faster than XGBoost on large datasets.

questions.question: What is CatBoost's key advantage over XGBoost and LightGBM when your dataset contains many high-cardinality categorical features like zip codes or product IDs?
options: CatBoost automatically reduces high-cardinality features to 10 unique values.

CatBoost natively handles categorical features using an ordered target encoding algorithm with mathematical guarantees against target leakage — no manual one-hot encoding or target encoding preprocessing is required. XGBoost and LightGBM require you to encode categoricals yourself, which is error-prone and can introduce data leakage without careful implementation.
CatBoost achieves lower training loss because it uses more trees by default.
CatBoost converts high-cardinality categories to embeddings like neural networks.
questions.answer: CatBoost natively handles categorical features using an ordered target encoding algorithm with mathematical guarantees against target leakage — no manual one-hot encoding or target encoding preprocessing is required. XGBoost and LightGBM require you to encode categoricals yourself, which is error-prone and can introduce data leakage without careful implementation.
questions.explanation: With 50,000 unique zip codes: One-hot encoding creates 50,000 sparse columns (slow, memory-hungry). Standard target encoding leaks if not done carefully in cross-validation. CatBoost handles both problems safely and automatically.

questions.question: What does 'early stopping' in XGBoost prevent, and how does it work?
options: Early stopping prevents the model from exceeding its memory limit.

Early stopping prevents overfitting from using too many trees. After each new tree is added, XGBoost evaluates performance on a held-out validation set. If the validation metric doesn't improve for N consecutive rounds (e.g., early_stopping_rounds=50), training stops — automatically choosing the optimal number of trees without overfitting.
Early stopping reduces training time by limiting each tree to 100 splits.
Early stopping prevents the learning rate from decreasing below a minimum value.
questions.answer: Early stopping prevents overfitting from using too many trees. After each new tree is added, XGBoost evaluates performance on a held-out validation set. If the validation metric doesn't improve for N consecutive rounds (e.g., early_stopping_rounds=50), training stops — automatically choosing the optimal number of trees without overfitting.
questions.explanation: Without early stopping, you set n_estimators=500 and risk overfitting after tree 200. With early stopping, training automatically stops at the optimal point (e.g., 247 trees) — training is faster AND the model generalizes better.
